name: Shaker
description: Shaker kitchens are defined by their clean-lined, recessed-panel door fronts, drawing on a timeless American craft tradition that translates beautifully into the British home. They sit equally well in period cottages and modern extensions, making them the most versatile and consistently popular kitchen style in the UK.
features: Recessed centre-panel door profile
Available painted or in natural wood finishes
Suits both traditional and contemporary interiors
Wide range of colour options across all retailers

name: Handleless
description: Handleless kitchens achieve their sleek, uninterrupted look through the use of integrated J-pull channels or push-to-open mechanisms in place of traditional handles. They are a staple of modern open-plan living spaces and pair naturally with high-gloss, matt lacquer, or textured slab door fronts.
features: Integrated J-pull or push-to-open mechanisms
Clean, uninterrupted door fronts
Ideal for open-plan kitchen-diners
Low maintenance with no handle hardware to clean

name: Modern
description: Modern kitchens prioritise clean lines, functional layouts, and a pared-back aesthetic that focuses attention on materials and finishes rather than decorative detail. They typically feature slab doors, integrated appliances, and a restricted colour palette of whites, greys, and neutral tones.
features: Flat slab door fronts with minimal detailing
Integrated appliances for a seamless look
Neutral colour palettes in gloss or matt finishes
Emphasis on ergonomic layout and storage efficiency

name: Traditional
description: Traditional kitchens celebrate classic British design, featuring raised or recessed panel doors, ornate cornicing, and decorative handles that evoke the charm of a well-established country home. They work particularly well in older properties and are most commonly specified in cream, sage green, and navy blue finishes.
features: Raised or recessed panel door profiles
Decorative cornicing and plinth detailing
Classic ceramic or cup-pull hardware
Suits Victorian, Edwardian, and period properties

name: Contemporary
description: Contemporary kitchens blend modern functionality with warmer materials and softer lines than a strictly minimalist approach, often mixing handleless uppers with shaker lowers or combining timber accents with painted cabinets. This style bridges the gap between ultra-modern and traditional, appealing to homeowners who want a current look without sacrificing warmth.
features: Mix of materials such as timber, stone, and lacquer
Flexible layouts combining different door profiles
Warmer tones including sage, navy, and warm white
Suits new-build and extended properties

name: German
description: German kitchens are manufactured to exacting engineering tolerances and typically feature precision-fit carcasses, soft-close hardware throughout, and advanced internal storage systems. Brands such as Häcker, Nobia, and Rotpunkt set the benchmark for quality in this segment, offering handleless and contemporary styles built to last decades.
features: Precision-engineered rigid carcasses
Comprehensive soft-close and full-extension drawer hardware
Advanced internal organisation systems
Long manufacturer warranties typically 10–25 years

name: Painted
description: Painted kitchens offer limitless colour flexibility, with door fronts available either factory-sprayed to a specific RAL or Farrow & Ball shade or hand-painted on site to match any interior palette. They are a perennial favourite in period homes and bring a bespoke quality to any renovation project.
features: Factory or on-site paint finish in any colour
Pairs beautifully with natural stone worktops
Popular in shaker and in-frame profiles
Easily refreshed with a repaint over time

name: Solid Wood
description: Solid wood kitchens use real timber for door fronts and sometimes carcasses, bringing natural warmth, grain variation, and a sense of craftsmanship that no laminate or foil wrap can replicate. Oak, walnut, and ash are the most popular species, and these kitchens are designed to improve with age as the wood develops its natural patina.
features: Real timber door fronts with natural grain variation
Ages and improves with proper care and maintenance
Available in oak, walnut, ash, and other species
Can be sanded and refinished if damaged

name: In-Frame
description: In-frame kitchens are characterised by doors and drawers that sit within a solid timber face-frame attached to the front of the carcass, replicating the construction method of traditional British furniture makers. This style is considered the pinnacle of traditional cabinetry and is most associated with high-end bespoke and semi-bespoke kitchen companies.
features: Doors sit within a solid timber face-frame
Considered the benchmark of traditional craftsmanship
Available in painted or natural wood finishes
Popular with Farrow & Ball and heritage colour palettes

name: Luxury
description: Luxury kitchens occupy the top tier of the market, combining premium materials such as marble, solid brass hardware, and hand-painted or veneered cabinetry with bespoke sizing and a fully personalised design service. Brands in this category include Neptune, DeVOL, and Smallbone of Devizes, and these projects typically involve close collaboration between client, designer, and installer over several months.
features: Bespoke or semi-bespoke sizing and configuration
Premium materials including stone, brass, and solid timber
Personalised design and project management service
Long lead times with meticulous on-site installation
